1: The superscription links Psalm 56 to 1 Samuel 21:10–15: David fled to Gath to escape Saul, was recognized as Israel's king, and feigned madness before Achish the king to secure his release. Achish's court servants identified him as "the king of the land" for whom Israel sang "Saul has killed his thousands and David his ten-thousands" (1 Sam 21:11) — making David's presence at the Philistine court immediately dangerous. The musical heading "To the tune 'Dove of the Far-off Oaks'" (ʿal-yônat ʾēlem rĕḥōqîm) is one of several such tune directions in the Psalter (cf. "Aijeleth Hashshahar" in Ps 22; "Shoshannim" in Pss 45, 69). The genre term miktam appears for Psalms 16 and 56–60; its meaning is debated — possibly "inscribed poem," "golden poem," or a technical term for a particular genre.
2: "My enemies trample on me all day long, for many are arrayed against me proudly" — the verb šāʾap (to trample, pant after with open mouth) describes aggressive pursuit with contemptuous intent. The phrase bĕgāʾôn (proudly, with arrogance) characterizes the enemies' attitude: not merely hostile but disdainful. In the honor-shame culture of the ancient Near East, public contempt was a weapon as powerful as physical threat — to be openly despised by one's enemies was to be stripped of social worth. David at the Philistine court had no social standing; he was entirely vulnerable to the contempt of those around him.
3: "When I am afraid, I will put my trust in you" — yôm ʾîrāʾ ʾănî ʾēlêkā ʾeḇṭaḥ. This is one of the most honest confessional formulas in the Psalter: fear is not denied but confronted as the context within which trust is exercised. The statement does not say "I will not be afraid" but "when I am afraid, I will trust" — the trust is a response to fear, not an absence of it. This is not a claim of superior courage but a commitment to reorient toward God even in the midst of genuine fear. Second Temple piety valued this honest acknowledgment of vulnerability alongside covenant loyalty.
4: "In God — whose word I praise — in God I trust; I will not be afraid. What can a mere human being do to me?" — the refrain structure (repeated in vv. 10–11) creates a liturgical response pattern suggesting public or communal use. Bāśār (flesh, human being) is the Psalter's standard contrast to ʾĕlōhîm (God): flesh is mortal, frail, and limited — what can such a being ultimately do to one who is under divine protection? The same contrast appears in Isaiah 31:3 ("The Egyptians are human and not God; their horses are flesh and not spirit") and 2 Corinthians 10:3–4. The word dāḇār (word) is praised — trust is in the reliability of what God has spoken, not merely in an abstract divine power.
5: "All day long they distort my words; all their scheming is against me for evil" — yĕʿaṣṣĕbû dĕbāray (they twist, distort my words). The verb ʿāṣab in this form means to cause pain or distortion — the enemies take the psalmist's own words and warp them into something that can be used against him. This is the specific danger of the court context: in a foreign court without advocates, anything said could be misrepresented to the king. Accusations based on distorted speech were a standard legal weapon in the ancient world, and the Psalter frequently invokes them (Pss 27:12; 35:11; 109:2).
6: "They gather themselves to stir up trouble, they lie in ambush, they track my steps as they wait to take my life" — the sequence describes coordinated surveillance and ambush. The term yāgûrû (they lie in ambush, gather to watch) combined with yišmĕrû ʿăqēbāy (they watch my heels/tracks) evokes the careful surveillance of a hunted person. "As they wait to take my life" (kaʾăšer qiwwû napšî) — the hunting metaphor is extended: they lie in ambush with focused patience, waiting to spring. This situation mirrors the Saul-era persecution David experienced before coming to Gath.
7: "For their crime, should they escape? In wrath cast down the peoples, O God!" — the prayer for divine justice against the violent. The question is rhetorical: crime should not go unpunished. "Cast down the peoples" (hôrēd ʿammîm) — the appeal is not merely for personal deliverance but for YHWH to bring down the wicked as covenant judge. The extension to "the peoples" suggests that the threat has widened beyond one court to a broader situation — the Psalter frequently moves from the individual's crisis to the universal scope of divine justice.
8: "You have recorded my restless wandering; store my tears in your wineskin — are they not written in your book?" — one of the most tender images in the Psalter. Nôd (leather flask or wineskin) is proposed as the vessel for God to store the psalmist's tears — a liquid record of grief. The image assumes that every tear is of account to God, that suffering is not invisible to the one who made the sufferer. "My wandering (nōdî)" uses the same root as the flask — a wordplay: the wandering and the vessel share etymology. "Your book" (bĕsipratĕkā) — the divine record book in which human lives and deeds are inscribed (cf. Exod 32:32–33; Ps 139:16; Mal 3:16). The divine record-keeping of tears became a rich image in later Jewish and Christian devotion.
9: "On the day I call out, my enemies will turn back; this I know: God is on my side" — zeh yādaʿtî kî ʾĕlōhîm lî (this I know, for God is for me). The confident assertion of divine alliance — not a wish but a known certainty. In ANE diplomatic language, "being on someone's side" was the language of treaty alliance and patron-client loyalty. The psalmist asserts that the great Suzerain has entered the field on his behalf — the enemies' attack is therefore an attack on the divine patron's client, which the patron must answer.
10: The refrain structure of vv. 10–11 repeats and slightly expands v. 4: "In God — whose word I praise — in the LORD — whose word I praise." The addition of the divine name YHWH alongside the general title ʾĕlōhîm intensifies the liturgical declaration. The doubling of the praise-formula emphasizes that it is the divine word (dāḇār) that is trustworthy — not merely the divine being in the abstract but the specific promises and covenants God has spoken. This is a creed-like affirmation of verbal revelation as the ground of trust.
11: "In God I trust; I will not be afraid. What can human beings do to me?" — the repeated refrain (cf. v. 4) closes the psalm's confession with a second hearing. The variation from v. 4 (bāśār, flesh → ʾādām, human being) shows a slightly different nuance: bāśār emphasizes mortality and physical weakness; ʾādām emphasizes creaturely status — both terms underline the radical disproportion between human threat and divine power. The question "what can they do?" is not naïve but theologically grounded: in light of divine protection, human hostility has a ceiling it cannot breach.
12: "I have made vows to you, O God, and I will fulfill them; I will offer you my thanksgiving" — the thanksgiving vow (neder) was a standard element of ancient Israelite prayer: in distress, one pledged a specific offering in exchange for divine rescue; upon rescue, one fulfilled that pledge publicly at the temple. This social-contractual dimension of prayer was not mere bargaining — it was a formal covenant exchange that publicly displayed the divine faithfulness when fulfilled. The thanksgiving offering (tôdāh) was a particular type of peace offering (Lev 7:12–15) accompanied by singing; Ps 56 itself may have been used as part of such a thanksgiving ceremony.
13: "For you have saved my life from death, and my feet from stumbling, so that I may walk before God in the light that belongs to the living" — the conclusion articulates the purpose of rescue: not mere survival but restored access to the divine presence (hithalēk lipnê ʾĕlōhîm, to walk before God). "The light that belongs to the living" (bĕʾôr haḥayyîm) is the condition of being alive in God's presence — the opposite of Sheol's darkness (Job 10:21–22; 38:17; Ps 88:12). The language echoes Job 33:30 ("to walk in the light of life") and anticipates John 8:12 ("I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness but will have the light of life"). Walking before God in the light is the full goal of the rescued life.
